reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。
request_prompt.user:
/no_think 下面是一个 FNM unresolved cluster。请只返回 JSON 数组，不要解释。 本次只允许动作：match、ignore_ref、needs_review。 若截图已经能明确判断坏掉的数字或同页注释，请直接输出可自动落地的动作，不要退回 needs_review。 { "cluster_id": "toc-ch-016-genderedmaniawomensmadne:toc-ch-016-genderedmaniawomensmadne:endnote", "chapter_title": "Gendered mania Women’s madness in late-imperial medicine", "page_range": [ 385, 422 ], "note_system": "endnote", "request_mode": "anchor_rebind", "allowed_actions": [ "match", "ignore_ref", "needs_review" ], "page_contexts": [ { "page_no": 40, "ocr_excerpt": "There were effectively no “types of madness” in Chinese medicine, just one madness—kuang or diankuang—with many symptoms. Historians usually interpret dian as a form of madness distinct from kuang (see 1: 5n9), but I will argue that a clear symptomatic distinction between dian and kuang only crystallized in the nineteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Kuang referred to various disorders of perception, affect, language, and especially behavior. People were labeled kuang when they lost some or all social competence—a woman ran outside naked, a man became delirious or took himself for a god. Physicians may have focused on patent mad behavior because they were only called to treat men and women whom their relatives saw as patently mad. Most feelings of mental discomfort were probably not reported to doctors, and thus rarely appeared in medical documents. I will call the symptoms of kuang “psycho-behavioral.” Although madness was defined socially, physicians usually interpreted insane behavior through categories that drew diagnosis away from the patient's daily context. Armed with notions such as \"stasis,\" \"depletion,\" or \"Liver Fire,\" they reduced to organic imbalances problems that had probably d ..." }, { "page_no": 385, "ocr_excerpt": "# Gendered mania? Women's madness in late-imperial case records A notorious adage circulating in late imperial medical circles declared that the women's illnesses were more difficult to cure than men's. $ ^{1} $ Two other sayings claimed that women's bodies were \"governed by Blood\" (以血為主 or a variant) and that treating women necessitated \"separate prescriptions\" (别方). Ming and Qing physicians cited these sayings to set up concurring arguments, to introduce objections or qualifications, or to clinch points with the help of a well-formulated piece of common knowledge. In this chapter, I want to explore whether the gender of the sufferer was one of the fault lines along which the understanding and treatment of mad acts and mad people were divided. Did physicians from the Song (960-1279) to the Qing (1644-1911) describe madness as occurring to women by different mechanisms than to men? Did the treatments they prescribed differ according to the gender of the patient? What other aspects of medical approaches to insanity were “gendered”? Since mad behavior was obviously less frequent than menses and pregnancy, relatively few cases of madness where the patient was a woman have survived. Ma ..." }, { "page_no": 386, "ocr_excerpt": "comments in medical treatises about how madness occurred to women. I selected cases from the twelfth to the early twentieth century that appeared in sections on madness of case collections, as well as other cases in which women were said to be suffering from kuang or dian or in which their behavior was described as kuang or dian, and finally cases that did not conform with the above criteria but in which the symptoms corresponded with those mentioned in descriptions of dian and kuang, that is, manic running, discarding of clothes, excessive anger or grief, and the like. Since it would be cumbersome to reinsert each case in its intellectual and social context, I will refer to relevant sections in other chapters for more information on the physicians who wrote these cases. I consider “postpartum madness” as distinct, and I therefore discuss it in its own section at the end of this chapter.
Table 9.1 Case records of female patients suffering from madness symptoms
Dates# ..." }, { "page_no": 388, "ocr_excerpt": "whether postpartum insanity was “really madness,” since late-imperial physicians tied it explicitly to what they called madness. ### 9.1 Non-gendered bodies, gendered etiologies Medical explanations of mad behavior in treatises purported to apply to both men and women. But in practice, did doctors consistently trace cases of women's mad behavior to the roots they identified in their treatises? Did their descriptions and treatments apply to the \"androgynous body\" (Furth 1999: 25-29, 44-48 and 56-57)—a body without anatomical sex—that characterized elite Chinese medicine? Charlotte Furth has indeed argued that late-imperial physicians focused increasingly less on the uniqueness of women, and tended to understand the women's illnesses in the same way as they understood men's. I shall agree with Furth in general, but I will also show that the etiology of madness was already ungendered in the Song dynasty (960-1279), and that glimpses of gendered etiologies only appeared in late-imperial times, especially in the nineteenth century. In recorded symptoms, identified etiology, and adopted therapy, a spectrum of more or less gendered solutions were available. Some symptoms (notably nudity a ..." }, { "page_no": 389, "ocr_excerpt": "In both etiology and treatment, extant cases of “mad women” predating 1500 are indistinguishable from those of men. Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154) gave the same pill to a woman who had been scared out of her wits by pirates and to a man who had lost his mind as he was about to be executed as a criminal. Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) cured a manic woman with a purgative he borrowed from the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{4} $ Two cases by Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) displayed the same gender neutrality. $ ^{5} $ If these physicians made distinctions between genders, they did not record them in their case files. Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, \"In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted\" (夫婦人顛狂病者 ..." }, { "page_no": 390, "ocr_excerpt": "In a brief case record, Chen explained that he cured a woman with the “Elixir that nourishes the correct” (養正丹), a common remedy for madness and seizures. $ ^{9} $ But as if to emphasize that this treatment was not designed exclusively for women, Chen mentioned that he also cured a man with the same remedy. Despite the popularity of Chen’s Excellent Recipes after Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559) re-edited them in 1547, there is no sign that Chen’s explanation of women’s mad behavior was ever adopted by elite physicians of the sixteenth century and after, probably because the Wind explanation that Chen had embraced had by then been replaced by a script that traced most problems involving consciousness to the disorderly movements of Fire and mucus in and around the Heart. $ ^{10} $ A few sixteenth-century cases tell us what therapies were current at the time. The physicians who designed them did not seem to think these remedies applied to women better than to men. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520s-30s) traced a young woman's strange behavior (she \"would cry or laugh, or run around naked, or shut the doors and speak a lot\" (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言) to \"harm caused by excessive ratiocination, [which made] the spiri ..." }, { "page_no": 392, "ocr_excerpt": "flow out in excess, Blood and Qi confuse the Heart; or when postpartum noxious dew flushes upward and makes speech jumbled and the mind unable to stay steadfast, this is the result of depletion of Blood and exhaustion of Spirit” (有婦人月水崩漏過多 血氣迷心 或產後惡露上沖 而言語錯亂 神志不守者 此血虛神耗也). $ ^{15} $ Li's popular work was among the first to ascribe mad symptoms to pathological menses, but the text explained how one specific imbalance could lead to insanity, not how mad symptoms should be interpreted in general. In other words, he was locking one etiology and some symptoms together in a contextually limited script. Li probably believed that the general explanation of insanity as caused by the action of Fire and mucus still applied to women who did not suffer from menorrhagia and whose symptoms did not occur postpartum. Although the rare sources we have are difficult to bind into a single narrative, dispersed writings indicate that at least some physicians thought that women suffered from madness for reasons particular to them. Zhuang Lüyan's 莊履嚴 Fuke baibian 婦科百辨 (early 17 $ ^{th} $ century?) claimed that the mucus that made women mad (顛狂) by encroaching on their Heart was brought about by extreme fr ..." }, { "page_no": 393, "ocr_excerpt": "薹憤 遂成此疾). $ ^{17} $ Sorrow appeared in both Zhuang's and Chen's accounts, but neither said much more about it than the lines I just cited. cluster.chapter_title: Gendered mania Women’s madness in late-imperial medicine

unmatched_anchors.source_text: Since mad behavior was obviously less frequent than menses and pregnancy, relatively few cases of madness where the patient was a woman have survived. Many arguments in this chapter are based on 35 case files—from 23 physicians—of female patients who suffered from mad symptoms (see Table 9.1 next page), as well as a few general
Table 9.1 Case records of female patients suffering from madness symptoms
Table 9.1 Case records of female patients suffering from madness symptoms

Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

In a brief case record, Chen explained that he cured a woman with the “Elixir that nourishes the correct” (養正丹), a common remedy for madness and seizures. $ ^{9} $ But as if to emphasize that this treatment was not designed exclusively for women, Chen mentioned that he also cured a man with the same remedy. Despite the popularity of Chen’s Excellent Recipes after Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559) re-edited them in 1547, there is no sign that Chen’s explanation of women’s mad behavior was ever adopted by elite physicians of the sixteenth century and after, probably because the Wind explanation that Chen had embraced had by then been replaced by a script that traced most problems involving consciousness to the disorderly movements of Fire and mucus in and around the Heart. $ ^{10} $

A few sixteenth-century cases tell us what therapies were current at the time. The physicians who designed them did not seem to think these remedies applied to women better than to men. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520s-30s) traced a young woman's strange behavior (she "would cry or laugh, or run around naked, or shut the doors and speak a lot" (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言) to "harm caused by excessive ratiocination, [which made] the spirit unable to hold to its lodging" (思慮過傷 神不守舍也). $ ^{11} $ In the sixteenth century, this etiology typified medicine's increasing focus on "inner harm"—a category that included exhaustion, food scarcity or excesses, and emotional imbalances—and the devising of new ways to treat it. Wu's therapy based on human placenta differed from those proposed in contemporary books, but, as great specialist of medicinal drugs Li Shizhen 李時珍 (1518-
或產後惡露上沖 而言語錯亂 神志不守者 此血虛神耗也). $ ^{15} $ Li's popular work was

flow out in excess, Blood and Qi confuse the Heart; or when postpartum noxious dew flushes upward and makes speech jumbled and the mind unable to stay steadfast, this is the result of depletion of Blood and exhaustion of Spirit” (有婦人月水崩漏過多 血氣迷心 或產後惡露上沖 而言語錯亂 神志不守者 此血虛神耗也). $ ^{15} $ Li's popular work was among the first to ascribe mad symptoms to pathological menses, but the text explained how one specific imbalance could lead to insanity, not how mad symptoms should be interpreted in general. In other words, he was locking one etiology and some symptoms together in a contextually limited script. Li probably believed that the general explanation of insanity as caused by the action of Fire and mucus still applied to women who did not suffer from menorrhagia and whose symptoms did not occur postpartum.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object "madness illness" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term "madness illness" by which Qing law
rs as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: There were effectively no “types of madness” in Chinese medicine, just one madness—kuang or diankuang—with many symptoms. Historians usually interpret dian as a form of madness distinct from kuang (see 1: 5n9), but I will argue that a clear symptomatic distinction between dian and kuang only crystallized in the nineteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Kuang referred to various disorders of perception, affect, language, and especially behavior. People were labeled kuang when they lost some or all social competence—a woman ran outside naked, a man became delirious or took himself for a god. Physicians may have focused on patent mad behavior because they were only called to treat men and women whom their relatives saw as patently mad. Most feelings of mental discomfort were probably not reported to doctors, and thus rarely appeared in medical documents. I will call the symptoms of kuang “psycho-behavioral.”

rebind_candidates.note_excerpt: 52a, “Women’s section” 婦人門, “Miscellaneous recipes for postpartum [disorders]” 產後雜方 (LiuChYQ: 434B). With “in a blanket manner,” I try to reflect the double meaning of shuai 率 as “comprehensively” and “haphazardly.”
rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: A woman who has a fit of dian does not know shame: she becomes candy-like $ ^{46} $ when she sees a man and very angry when she sees a woman. She will go as far as going naked and exposing her body. In such situations, Liver Fire is ardent and [the woman] thinks about a man but is unable to get one: this causes stasis and congealing that turn into dian. How does ardent Liver Fire turn into dian? It must be because, in women, Liver-Wood is flourishing: $ ^{47} $ Liver Fire coerces the Heart, causing the Heart’s ruler to descend from the palace. But the Heart’s Envelope keeps guard on the outside, so how can it let [the Heart, i.e. the ruler] be intimidated? $ ^{48} $ It is little known that Liver Fire is none other than depletion Fire. $ ^{49} $ Depletion Fire and Ministerial Fire are of the same sort. When [Fire?] associates with brigands, $ ^{50} $ it forgets [the ruler’s] sagely brilliance $ ^{51} $ and it directly sets the palace ablaze. But once the Heart-ruler walks out, why is there only dian and not death? This must be because Kidney Water comes to the rescue. Thinking of a man and being unable to get him is because the Kidneys are flourishing. Although this is Kidney Fire, Kidney Water is actually exhausted. When Liver Fire becomes coercive, the Heart [should be] provided for by Kidney Water: this is why there is only dian and not death. The treatment method is to purge the Liver root and replenish Kidney Water, as well as to channel static Qi. $ ^{52} $

rebind_candidates.note_excerpt: 8b, “Methods for balancing and replenishing” 調補法 (Chen YYQ: 211A).
rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Several cases by Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (fl. 1570s-1610s) indicate that one physician could propose many different explanations for similar behaviors, an aspect of late-imperial medicine with which we are now familiar. In two records published in 1576 and 1587, Gong treated female patients with the “Avert-wind tongsheng powder” 防風通聖散, a formula that a book by his father had claimed could “treat the suddenly erupting symptoms of all madness and Wind disorders” (治一切癲狂風疾 暴發之症).¹⁸ When he re-edited his father’s work, Gong added that this remedy “treats diankuang that has just started to arise” (此方治癲狂初起), when “in most cases there is repletion-Heat and Wind-heteropathy” (多有實熱風邪).¹⁹ In later books, Gong explained that women’s dian behavior (singing at strange times and “climbing on rooftops” 歌唱無時 簫垣上屋) was “caused by constructive Blood (?) lost in the Heart’s Envelope” (營血迷於心包所致也).²⁰ I do not know what Gong Tingxian meant by “constructive Blood,” but it may have been unique to women. As we have seen, however, blockage of the “Heart’s Envelope” was one of the most commonly identified causes of disorders of consciousness for both men and women in late-

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: There were effectively no “types of madness” in Chinese medicine, just one madness—kuang or diankuang—with many symptoms. Historians usually interpret dian as a form of madness distinct from kuang (see 1: 5n9), but I will argue that a clear symptomatic distinction between dian and kuang only crystallized in the nineteenth century. $ ^{5} $ Kuang referred to various disorders of perception, affect, language, and especially behavior. People were labeled kuang when they lost some or all social competence—a woman ran outside naked, a man became delirious or took himself for a god. Physicians may have focused on patent mad behavior because they were only called to treat men and women whom their relatives saw as patently mad. Most feelings of mental discomfort were probably not reported to doctors, and thus rarely appeared in medical documents. I will call the symptoms of kuang “psycho-behavioral.” Although madness was defined socially, physicians usually interpreted insane behavior through categories that drew diagnosis away from the patient's daily context. Armed with notions such as "stasis," "depletion," or "Liver Fire," they reduced to organic imbalances problems that had probably developed from social and interpersonal events. Perhaps because of doctors' emphasis on the organic roots of mad behavior, their chief therapeutic goal was usually to stop disabling symptoms rather tha ...

# Gendered mania? Women's madness in late-imperial case records A notorious adage circulating in late imperial medical circles declared that the women's illnesses were more difficult to cure than men's. $ ^{1} $ Two other sayings claimed that women's bodies were "governed by Blood" (以血為主 or a variant) and that treating women necessitated "separate prescriptions" (别方). Ming and Qing physicians cited these sayings to set up concurring arguments, to introduce objections or qualifications, or to clinch points with the help of a well-formulated piece of common knowledge. In this chapter, I want to explore whether the gender of the sufferer was one of the fault lines along which the understanding and treatment of mad acts and mad people were divided. Did physicians from the Song (960-1279) to the Qing (1644-1911) describe madness as occurring to women by different mechanisms than to men? Did the treatments they prescribed differ according to the gender of the patient? What other aspects of medical approaches to insanity were “gendered”? Since mad behavior was obviously less frequent than menses and pregnancy, relatively few cases of madness where the patient was a woman have survived. Many arguments in this chapter are based on 35 case files—from 23 physicians—of female patients who suffered from mad symptoms (see Table 9.1 next page), as well as a few general

comments in medical treatises about how madness occurred to women. I selected cases from the twelfth to the early twentieth century that appeared in sections on madness of case collections, as well as other cases in which women were said to be suffering from kuang or dian or in which their behavior was described as kuang or dian, and finally cases that did not conform with the above criteria but in which the symptoms corresponded with those mentioned in descriptions of dian and kuang, that is, manic running, discarding of clothes, excessive anger or grief, and the like. Since it would be cumbersome to reinsert each case in its intellectual and social context, I will refer to relevant sections in other chapters for more information on the physicians who wrote these cases. I consider “postpartum madness” as distinct, and I therefore discuss it in its own section at the end of this chapter.
Table 9.1 Case records of female patients suffering from madness symptoms
Dates# of casesPhysicians
1132

whether postpartum insanity was “really madness,” since late-imperial physicians tied it explicitly to what they called madness. ### 9.1 Non-gendered bodies, gendered etiologies Medical explanations of mad behavior in treatises purported to apply to both men and women. But in practice, did doctors consistently trace cases of women's mad behavior to the roots they identified in their treatises? Did their descriptions and treatments apply to the "androgynous body" (Furth 1999: 25-29, 44-48 and 56-57)—a body without anatomical sex—that characterized elite Chinese medicine? Charlotte Furth has indeed argued that late-imperial physicians focused increasingly less on the uniqueness of women, and tended to understand the women's illnesses in the same way as they understood men's. I shall agree with Furth in general, but I will also show that the etiology of madness was already ungendered in the Song dynasty (960-1279), and that glimpses of gendered etiologies only appeared in late-imperial times, especially in the nineteenth century. In recorded symptoms, identified etiology, and adopted therapy, a spectrum of more or less gendered solutions were available. Some symptoms (notably nudity and grief) were proportionally more common in women's cases, probably because these acts were seen as socially more problematic, and were therefore more likely to be brought to the attention of a physi ...

In both etiology and treatment, extant cases of “mad women” predating 1500 are indistinguishable from those of men. Xu Shuwei 許叔微 (1079-1154) gave the same pill to a woman who had been scared out of her wits by pirates and to a man who had lost his mind as he was about to be executed as a criminal. Wang Haogu 王好古 (13 $ ^{th} $ century) cured a manic woman with a purgative he borrowed from the Treatise on Cold Injury. $ ^{4} $ Two cases by Ni Weide 倪維德 (1303-1377) displayed the same gender neutrality. $ ^{5} $ If these physicians made distinctions between genders, they did not record them in their case files. Even one of the best-known Song works on women's illnesses proposed solutions that were equally applicable to men. Chen Ziming's 陳自明 All-Inclusive Excellent Recipes for Women (Furen daquan liangfang 婦人大全良方; hereafter Excellent Recipes), which he compiled in the mid thirteenth century, regrouped a large number of recipes designed to treat disorders as they happened to women. $ ^{6} $ On the issue that concerns us, Chen maintained that, "In women, the disorders dian and kuang are brought about by the deviant [Qi] of Wind, which is absorbed when Qi and Blood are depleted" (夫婦人顛狂病者 由血氣虛受風邪所為也). $ ^{7} $ Despite the mention of Blood depletion, this etiology was not gendered, because it corresponded with the traditional etiology of most Wind disorders. $ ^{8} $

In a brief case record, Chen explained that he cured a woman with the “Elixir that nourishes the correct” (養正丹), a common remedy for madness and seizures. $ ^{9} $ But as if to emphasize that this treatment was not designed exclusively for women, Chen mentioned that he also cured a man with the same remedy. Despite the popularity of Chen’s Excellent Recipes after Xue Ji 薛己 (1487-1559) re-edited them in 1547, there is no sign that Chen’s explanation of women’s mad behavior was ever adopted by elite physicians of the sixteenth century and after, probably because the Wind explanation that Chen had embraced had by then been replaced by a script that traced most problems involving consciousness to the disorderly movements of Fire and mucus in and around the Heart. $ ^{10} $ A few sixteenth-century cases tell us what therapies were current at the time. The physicians who designed them did not seem to think these remedies applied to women better than to men. Wu Qiu 吳球 (fl. 1520s-30s) traced a young woman's strange behavior (she "would cry or laugh, or run around naked, or shut the doors and speak a lot" (或哭或笑 或裸體而走 或閉戶而多言) to "harm caused by excessive ratiocination, [which made] the spirit unable to hold to its lodging" (思慮過傷 神不守舍也). $ ^{11} $ In the sixteenth century, this etiology typified medicine's increasing focus on "inner harm"—a category that included exhaustion, food scarcity ...

flow out in excess, Blood and Qi confuse the Heart; or when postpartum noxious dew flushes upward and makes speech jumbled and the mind unable to stay steadfast, this is the result of depletion of Blood and exhaustion of Spirit” (有婦人月水崩漏過多 血氣迷心 或產後惡露上沖 而言語錯亂 神志不守者 此血虛神耗也). $ ^{15} $ Li's popular work was among the first to ascribe mad symptoms to pathological menses, but the text explained how one specific imbalance could lead to insanity, not how mad symptoms should be interpreted in general. In other words, he was locking one etiology and some symptoms together in a contextually limited script. Li probably believed that the general explanation of insanity as caused by the action of Fire and mucus still applied to women who did not suffer from menorrhagia and whose symptoms did not occur postpartum. Although the rare sources we have are difficult to bind into a single narrative, dispersed writings indicate that at least some physicians thought that women suffered from madness for reasons particular to them. Zhuang Lüyan's 莊履嚴 Fuke baibian 婦科百辨 (early 17 $ ^{th} $ century?) claimed that the mucus that made women mad (顛狂) by encroaching on their Heart was brought about by extreme fright or sorrow (驚憂之極 痰犯心胸所 致), $ ^{16} $ an emotional theory of mucus that was still relatively rare at the time. In the late seventeenth century, Chen Shiduo 陳士鐸 argued that fits of madness caused by ...

薹憤 遂成此疾). $ ^{17} $ Sorrow appeared in both Zhuang's and Chen's accounts, but neither said much more about it than the lines I just cited. It is tempting to speculate that women isolated from their kin by patrilocal marriage and with limited social relations because of the necessity to stay at home were more likely than men to suffer from “sorrow.” Several cases by Gong Tingxian 龔廷賢 (fl. 1570s-1610s) indicate that one physician could propose many different explanations for similar behaviors, an aspect of late-imperial medicine with which we are now familiar. In two records published in 1576 and 1587, Gong treated female patients with the “Avert-wind tongsheng powder” 防風通聖散, a formula that a book by his father had claimed could “treat the suddenly erupting symptoms of all madness and Wind disorders” (治一切癲狂風疾 暴發之症).¹⁸ When he re-edited his father’s work, Gong added that this remedy “treats diankuang that has just started to arise” (此方治癲狂初起), when “in most cases there is repletion-Heat and Wind-heteropathy” (多有實熱風邪).¹⁹ In later books, Gong explained that women’s dian behavior (singing at strange times and “climbing on rooftops” 歌唱無時 簫垣上屋) was “caused by constructive Blood (?) lost in the Heart’s Envelope” (營血迷於心包所致也).²⁰ I do not know what Gong Tingxian meant by “constructive Blood,” but it may have been unique to women. As we have seen, however, blockage of the “Heart’s Envelope” ...
